coredata.dc:title: Study on preparation of UV-CDs/Zeolite-4A/TiO2 composite photocatalyst coupled with ultraviolet-irradiation and their application of photocatalytic degradation of dyes
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Jiaqi
Fan, Yuping
Dong, Xianshu
Zeng, Hongbo
Ma, Xiaomin
Fu, Yuanpeng
coredata.dc:description: In this work, ultraviolet irradiation was employed to assist in the preparation of a novel photocatalyst composite in the form of carbon dots/zeolite-4A/TiO2, using coal tailings as the source of silicon-aluminum and carbon. The composite was designed for the degradation of methylene blue under 500 W of UV light irradiation. Zeolite-4A was used as a support for the well-dispersed carbon dots and TiO2 nanoparticles. The as-prepared composites were subjected to thorough characterization, confirming the successful formation of zeolite-4A with a cube structure, along with the loading of TiO2 and coal-based CDs in the composites. The experimental results demonstrated that the UV-CZTs nanocomposites exhibited a remarkable removal efficiency of 90.63% within 90 min for MB. The corresponding rate constant was exceptionally high at 0.0331 min−1, surpassing that of the Dark-CZTs and pure TiO2. This significant enhancement was possibly due to the synergistic effect of adsorption photocatalysis of the UV-CZTs, combined with the excellent electron-accepting capabilities of the coal-based CDs, which led to highly improved charge separation. An investigation of the spent photocatalyst's recyclability revealed that it retained a remarkable 82.94% MB removal efficiency after five consecutive cycles, signifying the stability of the composite. Trapping experiments also elucidated the primary reactive species responsible for MB degradation, which were identified as photo-generated holes and ⸱O2 − species. By this process, the hydroxyl radicals generated in the system successfully promoted the transformation of coal tailings to coal-based zeolite and coal-based CDs. Coal-based zeolite served as an excellent carrier of titanium dioxide, which improved its dispersibility. The inhibition of e−-h+ recombination of titanium dioxide by introducing coal-based CDs improved the photocatalytic ability of titanium dioxide. Through this study, coal tailings, as a coal processing waste, were transformed into high-value materials, and relevant photocatalytic composite materials could be prepared with broad application prospects.

head.ce:title: Study on preparation of UV-CDs/Zeolite-4A/TiO2 composite photocatalyst coupled with ultraviolet-irradiation and their application of photocatalytic degradation of dyes

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, ultraviolet irradiation was employed to assist in the preparation of a novel photocatalyst composite in the form of carbon dots/zeolite-4A/TiO2, using coal tailings as the source of silicon-aluminum and carbon. The composite was designed for the degradation of methylene blue under 500 W of UV light irradiation. Zeolite-4A was used as a support for the well-dispersed carbon dots and TiO2 nanoparticles. The as-prepared composites were subjected to thorough characterization, confirming the successful formation of zeolite-4A with a cube structure, along with the loading of TiO2 and coal-based CDs in the composites. The experimental results demonstrated that the UV-CZTs nanocomposites exhibited a remarkable removal efficiency of 90.63% within 90 min for MB. The corresponding rate constant was exceptionally high at 0.0331 min−1, surpassing that of the Dark-CZTs and pure TiO2. This significant enhancement was possibly due to the synergistic effect of adsorption photocatalysis of the UV-CZTs, combined with the excellent electron-accepting capabilities of the coal-based CDs, which led to highly improved charge separation. An investigation of the spent photocatalyst's recyclability revealed that it retained a remarkable 82.94% MB removal efficiency after five consecutive cycles, signifying the stability of the composite. Trapping experiments also elucidated the primary reactive species responsible for MB degradation, which were identified as photo-generated holes and ⸱O2 − species. By this process, the hydroxyl radicals generated in the system successfully promoted the transformation of coal tailings to coal-based zeolite and coal-based CDs. Coal-based zeolite served as an excellent carrier of titanium dioxide, which improved its dispersibility. The inhibition of e−-h+ recombination of titanium dioxide by introducing coal-based CDs improved the photocatalytic ability of titanium dioxide. Through this study, coal tailings, as a coal processing waste, were transformed into high-value materials, and relevant photocatalytic composite materials could be prepared with broad application prospects.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Water purification has become a critical focus for academic researchers and industries, due to the escalating issues of pollution, posing a risk to accessible safe and clean water. Among the pollutants in contaminated water, dyes from industrial wastewater pose a significant challenge, as they cannot be easily removed, are almost completely non-biodegradable, and highly toxic (Rachna et al., 2018). Therefore, cost-effective treatment methods are needed to address these issues. In recent decades, photocatalysis has emerged as a promising technology with a wide range of environmental applications, especially in wastewater treatment, due to its simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and sustainability (Fajrina and Tahir, 2019; Bie et al., 2021). Titanium dioxide (TiO2) has been widely used in various fields due to its non-toxic and chemically stable characteristics (Zhang et al., 2019a, 2019b; Lee and Park, 2013; Chen et al., 2015; Ismael, 2021), especially in the context of photocatalytic decolorization or the degradation of organic contaminants. When exposed to ultraviolet (UV) light, TiO2 can degrade organic pollutants, leading to total mineralization by generating powerful oxidants (Institute of Biochemistry et al., 2019; Hussain et al., 2021; Mohd Adnan et al., 2019). However, the photocatalytic activity and effective utilization of TiO2 are impeded by its rapid recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs (EHPs), as well as particle aggregation and inefficient recovery (TiO2 photocatalysis and related surface, 2008; Chen et al., 2016; Liang et al., 2018).

Many approaches have been used to improve the photocatalytic activity of TiO2, with some involving the introduction of electron acceptors to inhibit the recombination of photogenerated EHPs (Nasr et al., 2018; Song et al., 2018; Xing et al., 2016), and the preparation of immobilized photocatalysts to provide high adsorption capacity and abundant active sites, due to their high surface area and porous characteristics (Srikanth et al., 2017; Uricchio et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2023). Coal-based carbon materials and coal-derived mineral materials from low-quality coal sources have received substantial global attention as multifunctional materials, notably coal tailings, a type of coal-based solid waste rich in minerals associated with the coal flotation process, when combined with TiO2 to enhance its photocatalytic performance (Dong et al., 2014; Li et al., 2015; Mushtaq et al., 2019; Supelano García et al., 2022). Among various coal-based and coal-derived materials, coal-based carbon dots (CDs) and coal ash–based zeolites have been investigated in the field of photocatalytic degradation. For example, Bo et al. (Zhang et al., 2017) used raw coal to produce coal-based CDs using a mixed acid/ultrasound hybrid method. These CDs were subsequently combined with in-situ synthesized TiO2 particles to form CDs/TiO2 nanoparticles. When employed as a photocatalyst for degrading pulping black liquor, the CDs/TiO2 composite exhibited an impressive CODcr elimination rate of 81.2% after 3 h of treatment, outperforming both pure TiO2 and coal-based C-dots in terms of visible-light photocatalytic effectiveness.

Many successful efforts have been made to produce zeolite from biowaste and low-cost waste, such as palm ash (Khanday et al., 2017a, 2017b; Khanday and Hameed, 2018) and sepiolite (Marrakchi et al., 2016). One study prepared a novel ternary composite in the form of an Ag3PO4/TiO2/molecular sieve (MS) using in-situ deposition and hydrothermal growth, with coal gangue as the raw material for the molecular sieve (zeolite P) (Bian et al., 2020). This ternary composite displayed excellent removal rates of 97.9% and 96.7% for formaldehyde and SIBX, respectively. The hybrid structure of zeolite P and Ag3PO4–TiO2 contributed to improved photo-absorption and significantly suppressed the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers due to the three-fold introduction of TiO2. Moreover, the photocatalyst's reusability was evaluated by successive reuse, and even after five reuses, the elimination rates remained high at 97.3% and 94.6% for formaldehyde and SIBX after more than 105 min, respectively. Traditionally, the synthesis of coal-based zeolites has involved hydrothermal crystallization in a strongly basic medium (Zhang et al., 2022).

Hydroxide ions (OH−) are known to catalyze the polymerization and depolymerization of aluminate and silicate species (Wang et al., 2021). Yu et al., in 2016 (Feng et al., 2016) found that ·OH radicals, as potent oxidant species, could effectively improve the formation of Si–O–Si bonds to promote the polymerization of aluminosilicate anions around hydrated cation species. This process significantly accelerated zeolite crystallization and reduced the alkalinity of the synthesis system by substituting OH− with ·OH. UV irradiation has been commonly used to generate ·OH radicals. High-energy irradiation can also effectively break down water, resulting in the production of ·OH radicals (Xu and Chance, 2007; Gligorovski et al., 2015). Furthermore, ·OH radicals can be continuously and more readily obtained by using UV irradiation with heterogeneous photocatalysts, such as TiO2, significantly enhancing the efficiency of ·OH radical generation (Fujioka et al., 2017; Agustina et al., 2005).

Based on the aforementioned theory, in this study, we introduced a novel method for preparing the composite photocatalyst. The precursor mixture of UV-CZTs was prepared by using coal tailings as the silicon-aluminum and carbon source. This mixture was subsequently aged in a 2 M NaOH solution in the presence of TiO2 under ultraviolet irradiation conditions. This alkali and oxidizing system promoted the nucleation process of coal-based zeolites, where the hydroxyl radicals in the system could transform the carbon structure of the coal tailings into smaller fragments, to produce CDs. Subsequently, a hydrothermal method was employed to generate the zeolite phase and load CDs and TiO2 onto its surface. The structure, morphology, and photocatalytic performance of the resulting UV-CZTs were then comprehensively investigated.

Hydroxyl radicals can not only target clay minerals in coal tailings, but also react with a small portion of the aromatic ring carbon in coal tailings, resulting in the formation of CDs, which has been verified by previous reports in the literature (Yue et al., 2019; Xiong et al., 2020). A series of characterizations confirmed the successful doping of CDs into the coal-based zeolite lattice, with a few CDs also bonding to the surface of TiO2. These coal-based CDs can play a crucial role as electron acceptors and photosensitive materials, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic performance of TiO2. Therefore, UV-CZTs can achieve superior performance when used for wastewater treatment. This study not only presents a technical pathway for the development of multifunctional photocatalysts with synergistic effects and superior performance, but also offers valuable insights into the comprehensive utilization of low-quality coal resources.

Residual waste materials from coal flotation in central Shanxi Province, China, were used as the source of the raw coal tailings. Industrial analysis revealed that the coal tailings consisted of 29.9 wt% organic carbon and 70.1 wt% inorganic minerals. Titanium (IV) oxide nano-powder (anatase, 98%) was purchased from Acros (Fairlawn, NJ, USA). Sodium hydroxide and methylene blue (MB) dye were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich (India). All reagents used in this experiment were of analytical reagent grade and directly employed without additional purification. Deionized water was utilized in all experiments. The samples underwent irradiation during the aging process using five ultraviolet lamp tubes with a central wavelength of 365 nm.

In the experiment, the raw coal tailings underwent a series of steps to prepare them for further analysis. First, the raw coal tailings were screened through a 120-mesh sieve to remove any large particles, and then the tailings were treated with 0.1 mol/L hydrochloric acid to eliminate impurities. Afterward, the tailings were washed until the pH reached 7 ± 0.1. The tailings were then dried and subjected to calcination at 800 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere for 1 h under a heating rate of 5 °C/min. The resulting product was designated as CTs. The main compositions of the CTs, which were assessed by an X-ray fluorescence spectrometer (XRF), are shown in Table 1 .

First, 2.0 g of TiO2 was added to 30 mL of sodium hydroxide solution (2 M) and mixed thoroughly under a stirring speed of 400 rpm. Then, 2.0 g of coal tailings were added to the mixture and stirred for 10 min. After dispersal in the solution, oxygen, which acted as an electronic scavenger, was introduced and maintained until saturated. The resulting mixture was then placed in a UV reaction lightbox, followed by exposure to a radiation intensity of 20 mW/cm2 for 16 h. After UV light exposure, the mixture was transferred into a hydrothermal reactor and maintained at 120 °C for 24 h. Then, the product was subjected to centrifugation, rinsed with deionized water until it became neutral, and finally freeze-dried for 48 h. This final sample was labeled as UV-CDs/zeolite/TiO2 (UV-CZTs). For comparison, a control sample denoted as Dark-CDs/zeolite/TiO2 (Dark-CZTs) was prepared using the same method, however, the UV light was turned off during the aging process. The detailed preparation process of the composites is shown in Fig. 1 .

In order to determine the existence of coal-based CDs after UV irradiation in the progress of preparation of UV-CZTs. According to the preparation method of UV-CZTs, after UV irradiation, the supernatant was separated from the mixture by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 15 min and adjusted to neutral using 0.1 M hydrochloric acid. The obtained supernatant was filtered using 0.22 μm filter paper and placed into a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 3 days to obtain an aqueous solution of coal-based CDs. Then, the aqueous solution of coal-based CDs was freeze-dried to obtain the final coal-based CDs product.

The structural and crystalline properties of the Dark-CZTs and UV-CZTs composites were analyzed using various techniques. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted on a D8 Venture detector (Bruker, USA) in reflection geometry with Cu–K radiation (λ = 0.154,056 nm) at 15 mA and 40 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements of the Dark-CZTs and UV-CZTs were carried out using a Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi instrument (XPS, Thermo Fisher-VG Scientific, USA) with an Al-Kα X-ray source at 20 eV and 200 W. Vacuum pressure during the tests was varied between 10−10 and 10−9 Torr, and the C1s binding energy (BE) was calibrated at 284.8 eV. Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) was performed using a Zeiss Sigma emission scanning electron microscope (SEM) equipped with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS, Brucker XFlash 6I30). The morphological and microstructural features were further examined by a JEOL JEM 2100 transmission electron microscope (TEM). The absorption edges of the Dark-CZTs/UV-CZTs/pure TiO2 composites were determined using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UH4150, HITACHI, Japan), with BaSO4 employed as the reflectance standard. Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms were determined using an ASAP 2460 nitrogen adsorption apparatus (Micromeritics, USA), and pore size distribution plots were constructed using the density functional theory (DFT) model, which was considered a suitable method for evaluating the micropores as well as mesopores. Chemical bonds in the TNT-MCTs composites were assessed by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS50). Finally, Raman microscopy was performed using a Raman Microscope (model CRM200 WITec, Ulm, Germany).

A UV light lamp with a power of 500 W and a center wavelength of 365 nm served as the light source. The reactor was loaded with 200 mL of methylene blue (MB, C16H18N3SCl, 10 mg/L) solution and 20 mg of catalyst. Before initiating illumination, the reaction mixture was stirred in the dark for 30 min to establish adsorption-desorption equilibrium, and the first specimen was collected as a control. Subsequently, the solution was subjected to vigorous stirring and exposed to UV light. Sampling was performed every 10 min, followed by centrifugation and filtration to eliminate the photocatalyst particles. The absorbance of the samples (An) was then detected using a UV–vis spectrophotometer at 664 nm, and both the adsorption capacities (qe, mg/g) and removal percentages (θ) were determined by:

The calibration curves were generated by plotting the absorbance against the concentrations of the dyes, according to the principles of the Beer-Lambert law.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability and recyclability tests of the photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The ability to be reused or recycled was investigated for the synthesized photocatalysts and compared to the pure TiO2 photocatalyst, considering the decolorization of MB under identical environmental conditions (90 min of continuous dark adsorption and UV irradiation). After each cycle, the photocatalysts were retrieved, washed with deionized water, centrifuged, and dried before they were used again in the next cycle. The tests were conducted for five cycles to evaluate and compare the reusability or recyclability of the synthesized photocatalysts and pure TiO2.

The XRD pattern of the post-pretreated CTs and CDs is displayed in Fig. 2 (a), indicating that it was mainly comprised of mullite, quartz, aluminosilicate, and graphitic carbon with a sp2 structure. The as-prepared coal-based CDs solution was blue-green under UV light, as shown in the inset of Fig. 2(a). The coal-based CDs have wide diffraction peaks at around 24.3°corresponding to the (002) crystal face of graphite (Zhang et al., 2017). This further confirms the successful preparation of coal-based carbon dots during UV irradiation. and the XRD patterns of the Dark-CZTs and UV-CZTs are depicted in Fig. 2(b). The well-resolved diffraction peaks of both synthesized composite photocatalysts at 25.3°, 37.0°, 37.9°, 38.7°, 48.2°, 53.9°, 55.2°, 62.6°, 68.9°, 70.3°, and 75.1° revealed that the photocatalyst had an anatase structure (JCPDS card No.00–021-1272). In addition, the diffraction peaks of 7.1°, 10.1°, 12.4°, 16.1°, 17.6°, 21.6°, 24.0°, 27.1°, 29.9°, and 34.1° were attributed to the zeolite-4A structure (Na92Al92Si100O384) (JCPDS card 43–0142). Analysis also revealed a high level of crystallinity. However, the distinct characteristic peak of CDs in the UV-CZTs was not detected by XRD due to their low crystallinity and high dispersion. The presence of TiO2 also potentially influenced the diffraction peaks of CDs. In UV-CZTs, only one weak and broad peak was observed at 24.3°, which could be attributed to the conjugate sp2 carbon structure of the (002) planes of the graphite lattice, and this was associated with the aromatic ring structure (Jia et al., 2020). This peak slightly shifted from the original graphite peak (26.4°), mainly due to the influence of oxygen-containing functional groups and the interactions among the different components in the composite material.

We conducted SEM analysis to visualize the morphological features of the samples. In the SEM diagram, we observed that the CTs mainly consisted of irregular particles, which could be attributed to coal-based lamellar aluminosilicate minerals and carbonaceous material (Fig. 3 (a)(b)). In comparison, the UV-CZTs (Fig. 3(e)(f)) showed a significant number of well-structured cubic crystals, as a typical morphology of zeolite-4A. This was dissimilar to the Dark-CZTs (Fig. 3(c)(d)) and characterized by 4 A zeolite structures with poor crystallinity, with reduced loading of TiO2. This indicated that the ·OH radicals, accelerated by UV light, contributed to the formation of the coal-based zeolite-4A phase. The zeolite-4A crystals were cubic and ranged in size from 1.8 to 2 μm. The zeolite-4A surface appeared smooth with well-defined edges, and was loaded with abundant TiO2 nanoparticles measuring about 0.1–0.2 μm in size (Fig. 3(f)). This observation suggested that the ultraviolet irradiation generates numerous ·OH free radicals on the surface of TiO2, which in turn promoted the generation of zeolite-4A from CTs particles. Furthermore, a strong interaction was inferred at the interface of the TiO2 and zeolite-4A particles, which increased the load of TiO2 and inhibited the agglomeration of the TiO2 particles. The elemental mapping pattern of the UV-CZTs confirmed the presence of C, O, Si, Al, and Ti (Fig. 3(g)–(l)). These elements were evenly distributed, and the content (wt.%) values of C, O, Si, Al, and Ti were 22.31, 38.03, 10.31, 10.01, and 19.34 for the UV-CZTs, respectively, and 24.47, 40.54, 9.02, 8.40, and 17.57 for the Dark-CZTs, respectively. The results indicated that UV irradiation of the TiO2 surfaces resulted in the generation of hydroxyl free radicals. These radicals facilitated the incorporation of silicon and aluminum into the zeolite 4 A framework, enhancing its structural composition. The higher Ti content in UV-CZTs also indicates that the load of TiO2 is greater.

The crystal structure of the UV-CZTs was examined by TEM. Fig. 4 (a) shows the morphologies of the composite structures of the zeolite-4A and TiO2 microspheres. The SAED patterns (insert image of Fig. 4(a)) of the UV-CZTs exhibited bright polymorphic rings for both TiO2 and zeolite-4A, confirming the clear polycrystallinity of the two nanoparticles. As shown in Fig. 4(b), numerous minuscule, opaque, and uniformly sized black particles were observed, indicating the excellent monodispersity features of the coal-based CDs. Upon enlarging Fig. 4(b), spherical nanoparticles in the form of as-prepared coal-based CDs were clearly visible (Fig. 4(c)). Lattice spacing was determined as 0.23 nm, which closely matched the reported value (0.25 nm) indexed to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon in the CQDs (Zhang et al., 2016a). The particle size of the coal-based CDs was less than 10 nm. Furthermore, the CDs were deposited on the surfaces of the UV-CZTs. The presence of TiO2 (anatase phase) was indicated by the lattice plane (101) with d-spacing of 0.35 nm in the UV-CZTs. The TEM results further verified the formation of the CDs/zeolite-4A/TiO2 composite structures, which also correspond with the XRD results.

XPS was used to further verify that TiO2 and CDs were successfully grafted onto the caudal coal-based zeolite-4A particle. The XPS survey scan (Fig. 5 (a) (b)) indicated that a significant amount of O (38.27 wt %), C (21.78 wt %), Ti (19.05 wt %), Si (11.71 wt %), and Al (9.19 wt %) were present on the UV-CZTs surface. A slightly different composition was found on the Dark-CZTs surface, and the percentages of O (39.6 wt %), C (24.19 wt %), Ti (17.13 wt %), Si (10.98 wt %), and Al (8.09 wt %) were similar to the SEM analysis results. Compared to the Dark-CZTs, the UV-CZTs showed increased levels of Si and Al, suggesting that the ·OH free radicals generated on the TiO2 surface under UV irradiation could incorporate more Si and Al into the zeolite-4A lattice (Zhang et al., 2023), which was consistent with the SEM results. High-resolution curve fitting of the C1s spectra of the UV-CZTs and Dark-CZTs displayed similar curve types (Fig. 5(c), (d)). The results revealed wide and asymmetric peaks representing four components with BE values of 284.8, 286.2, 287.59, and 289.77 eV, corresponding to sp2 hybridized (CC) carbon, epoxy/hydroxyl (C–O-) carbon, carbonyl (CO) carbon, and carboxyl (O–CO) carbon, respectively. The presence of CDs in UV-CZTs was further confirmed by the high-resolution C1s spectral curves. Interestingly, compared to the Dark-CZTs, the UV-CZTs showed a higher relative content of sp2 carbon (CC), indicating that during the process of irradiation aging, the ·OH free radicals generated on the TiO2 surface under UV light preferred to shear sp2 carbon within the aromatic ring and form C-dots rather than react with its edge and form oxygen-containing functional groups. This also verified that the C-dots acted as excellent electron acceptors and were further photo-reduced after their formation (Nagarjuna et al., 2015; Li et al., 2021). The O1s spectrum was split into three peaks corresponding to the BEs of C–O/Si–O (533.95 eV), CO/Al–O (532.41 eV), and metal oxide (530.98 eV) (Fig. 5(e), (f)). This indicated that oxygen was mainly present in the form of silico-aluminate, namely, zeolite-4A and TiO2. Additionally, certain oxygen-containing functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl), were found on the prepared composite photocatalyst surfaces. The illustrations in Fig. 5(g), (h) displayed the high-resolution XPS spectral data of Ti2p, indicating two distinct peaks at 459.57 and 465.4 eV, which corresponded to the TiO2 electron BEs Ti2p3/2 and Ti2p1/2, respectively. These findings aligned well with the XRD test results, confirming that TiO2 in both the UV-CZTs and Dark-CZTs composite particles was anatase-type TiO2 with high purity (Zhang et al., 2014).

The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 6 ) verified the existence of various functional groups on the surface of both as-prepared UV-CZTs and Dark-CZTs samples. The band observed within the 3200–3500 cm−1 spectrum could be ascribed to the O–H stretching and surface COOH groups on the composite photocatalysts. These groups were considered crucial in the photocatalytic process as they could capture the photogenerated electrons and holes, leading to the production of reactive oxygen species (Hassan et al., 2014; Pang and Abdullah, 2013). The weak bands observed around 1652.43 cm−1 were assigned to the CC functional groups in the aromatic ring of CDs. Moreover, obvious absorption peaks at 977.18 cm−1 in the UV-CZTs were partly attributed to the stretching vibration of certain minerals, including Si–O–C and Si–O–Si. The peak position of the composite photocatalysts shifted to a lower wavenumber (1050.59 cm−1 → 977.18 cm−1) compared to the CTs. Furthermore, the absorption intensity at 977.18 cm−1 of UV-CZTs was greater than that of the Dark-CZTs, indicating the possibility of stronger hydrogen bonding within the UV-CZTs due to the formation of zeolite-4A. The peaks in the 977.18 cm−1 region were also ascribed to the anti-symmetric stretching vibration of Si–O–Ti, implying a strong interaction between zeolite-4A and TiO2 (Huang et al., 2008). The shoulder peak at 778.93 cm−1 observed in the CTs was attributed to the Si–O bonds of quartz, which were not present in the composite photocatalysts. This indicated that the quartz phase was converted into the zeolite-4A phase. Additionally, the sharp band at 537.89 cm−1 corresponded to the aromatic C–H out-of-plane bending vibrations, providing additional evidence of CDs formation.

Raman scattering can offer greater sensitivity than XRD when characterizing the structure of carbon materials. Therefore, Raman spectroscopy was employed to analyze the different samples. As depicted in Fig. 7 , Dark-CZTs and UV-CZTs both exhibited distinct peaks at 146, 396, 515, and 636 cm−1, which corresponded to the E1g, B1 g, A1 g, and Eg modes of anatase TiO2, respectively. Moreover, additional peaks at 1349 and 1586 cm−1 were detected, which corresponded to the D-band and G-band of the carbon structures. The ID/IG index has been widely used to measure the quality of synthesized carbon nanostructures, commonly associated with their defect density (Barros et al., 2004; Pimenta et al., 2007; Wang et al., 1990). The ID/IG ratios for the different materials followed the order of 0.88 (CTs) > 0.87 (Dark-CZTs) > 0.83 (UV-CZTs). The results implied that UV irradiation possibly caused a partial decrease in disorder and an improvement in the ordered region. The decrease in ID/IG ratio in the UV-CZTs was a direct consequence of the enhanced conversion of the carbon atoms from sp3 to sp2 hybridization (Lueking et al., 2007). This strengthened the notion that the CDs underwent a moderate reduction, leaving a considerable portion of the carbon in an oxidized state. The Raman results further demonstrated that UV irradiation progressively improved the structure of the sp2 hybrid carbon atoms in the synthesized composite photocatalysts in the presence of TiO2. This verified that the aromatic carbon of the CTs initially transformed into the CDs by reacting with the ·OH radicals, and the CDs were subsequently photo-reduced by the photogenerated electrons. Thus, the CDs served as effective acceptors for photogenerated electrons, inhibiting the recombination of EHPs and thereby enhancing photocatalytic performance.

As shown in Fig. 8 (a), the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of the CTs, UV-CZTs, and Dark-CZTs exhibited IV type isotherms with adsorption hysteresis loops at relative pressures between 0.4 and 1.0. This indicated the presence of capillary condensation and suggested that these materials possessed a mesoporous structure (Miao et al., 2016). The observed hysteresis loop was classified as H4 type, which has often been found in certain mesoporous zeolite-4A and micro-mesoporous carbon materials. This implied that the prepared composite material contained slit pores. Fig. 8(b) displays the corresponding pore size distribution curves of the different samples, indicating that the pore size distribution curves of the UV-CZTs closely resembled that of the Dark-CZTs, with the most probable aperture located at around 4.5 nm. The pore structure characteristics of the related materials are listed in Table 2 . The surface area of the CTs was measured as 237.552 m2/g, while the surface areas of TiO2, Dark-CZTs, and UV-CZTs were determined as 10.829, 88.173, and 90.037 m2/g, respectively. After UV irradiation, the specific surface area of the UV-CZTs was higher compared to the Dark-CZTs. This increase in surface area could be attributed to the changes in pore structure due to crystal phase transition during sample preparation.

The light-response properties of all synthesized materials were investigated by performing UV–Vis DRS analysis. As shown in Fig. 9 (a), the UV-CZTs exhibited obvious redshifts and remarkably enhanced absorption over the entire UV–visible light range compared to pure TiO2 and Dark-CZTs. The Eg values of the samples were calculated using the Kubelka-Munk transformation (Eq. (3)) (Ye et al., 2017; Zhai et al., 2007):
(3)
(
α h v
)
n
= A
(
h v −
E g
)
,
where α represents the absorption coefficient, hv denotes the light frequency, A is a constant, and Eg indicates the band gap. The value of n was either 2 or ½, depending on whether the transitions were direct or indirect. The Tauc plots (Fig. 9(b)) were obtained using the direct transition approach and the Eg values of TiO2, Dark-CZTs, and UV-CZTs were estimated as 3.3, 3.29, and 3.26 eV, respectively. Compared to the Dark-CZTs, the UV-CZTs only exhibited slightly lower Eg values, indicating that the electronic band structure of TiO2 in the composite photocatalysts remained largely unaffected after UV irradiation. Therefore, changes in the optical features of UV-CZTs were likely due to three main reasons: (1) the dispersion of TiO2 in the zeolite-4A matrix possibly promoted the utilization of UV–visible light; (2) the CDs in the UV-CZTs could capture the photogenerated electrons, inhibiting the recombination of EHPs; (3) the coal-based CDs in the composite photocatalysts possibly improved visible light absorption efficiency, generating a larger number of photogenerated excitons necessary for photocatalytic reactions, leading to excellent degradation and mineralization properties (Liang et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation test on MB and photocatalytic kinetics
ce:section.ce:para:
The photocatalytic performance of the as-prepared nanocomposites was assessed and compared with the CTs and pure TiO2 by studying the photodegradation of MB (Fig. 10 (a)). The MB adsorption results for the four samples in the dark showed that an absorption-desorption balance was achieved between the materials in approximately 30 min. About 18.06% and 24.54% of MB were adsorbed in the dark by the Dark-CZTs and the UV-CZTs nanocomposites, respectively, within this 30-min period. This proved that accelerating the formation of zeolite-4A phases by the free radicals generated on the TiO2 surface through UV irradiation enhanced the adsorption performance of the UV-CZTs. In the presence of the UV-CZTs nanocomposites, the degradation rate of MB reached 90.63% under UV light after 60 min, while only 61.16% and 43.43% of MB were degraded using the Dark-CZTs and pure TiO2, respectively. This demonstrated that the UV-CZTs photocatalyst was a dual-functional material with outstanding capacity for both adsorption and photocatalytic functionality. The Langmuir-Hinshelwood kinetic model was employed to conduct kinetic studies for determining and estimating the photodegradation rate constant of MB (Wang et al., 2008). Most photocatalytic reactions can be simplified and modeled as pseudo first-order kinetic equations (Meng et al., 2013), and the order of equations is shown in Eqs. (4) and (5):
(4)

where Rate indicates the degradation rates of MB (mg/L·h), C denotes the concentration at a specific time t (h), C 0 represents the initial concentration of the reactant (mg/L), k is the reaction rate constant (h−1), K is the adsorption coefficient (L/min) of the reactant onto the catalyst particles, and
k

is the pseudo first-order rate constant (min−1). Fig. 10(b) displays the plot of kinetic models of UV-CZTs, Dark-CZTs, and TiO2 for the degradation of MB with correlation coefficients R2 of 0.9938, 0.9952, and 0.9988, respectively, indicating that the experimental data fit perfectly with the pseudo first-order kinetic model. The pseudo first-order rate constants of the Dark-CZTs and TiO2, and UV-CZTs, were 0.0124 and 0.0331 min−1, respectively. The enhanced photocatalytic performance of the UV-CZTs could be attributed to the inhibition of carrier recombination by the coal-based CDs and the synergistic effect of adsorption-photocatalysis.

The ability to recycle photocatalysts is critical for their practical use (Shao et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2016b). To assess this, a series of recycling tests was conducted by monitoring its conversion efficiency over five consecutive recycle tests, according to our previous work (Guo et al., 2021). After each photocatalytic test, the prepared photocatalysts were collected, followed by centrifuging, rinsing, drying, and recycling for the next run. Fig. 11 (a) displays the mass of the remaining photocatalysts recovered after each cycle, and the results indicated that the UV-CZTs demonstrated the lowest mass loss after five recycling experiments, demonstrating the best recycling performance. As shown in Fig. 11(b), after five consecutive tests, the MB decolorization rate only slightly changed for UV-CZTs (82.94% of MB decolorization), but markedly decreased for Dark-CZTs (38.16% of MB decolorization), and dramatically decreased for pure TiO2 (8.43% of MB decolorization). Fig. 11(c) and (d) shows the SEM images of the UV-CZTs and Dark-CZTs after five cycles. According to the results, a considerable amount of TiO2 loading remained on the surface of the UV-CZTs after five cycles. However, almost no TiO2 was loaded on the Dark-CZTs, which verified the strong interaction forces between TiO2 and zeolite-4A of the UV-CZTs, limiting TiO2 to the zeolite surface. In summary, the UV-CZTs exhibited the best stability and reusability due to the effective confinement of TiO2 on the zeolite-4A surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching experiments of the active species
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the degradation mechanism and assess the presence of active species during photocatalysis, radical quenching tests were conducted under identical conditions. Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA), tertiary-butyl (TBA), and benzoquinone were employed to quench h+, ·OH, and ·O2 −, respectively. Solutions of these scavengers at a concentration of 10−3 M were prepared and mixed with the MB solution, followed by a degradation test. The degradation efficacy was assessed and juxtaposed for each scenario against the degradation efficacy without scavengers. As shown in Fig. 12 , the addition of TBA to the photocatalytic system only slightly weakened the removal ability of the UV-CZTs nanocomposite, suggesting that ·OH was not the main species associated with the photocatalytic system. However, the degradation efficiency of MB was significantly attenuated following the addition of EDTA and benzoquinone into the photocatalytic system. This implied that ·O2 − and h+ played a crucial role in the photocatalytic degradation process.

Combining the aforementioned results, a plausible mechanism for the photocatalytic degradation of MB by the UV-CZTs was determined, as illustrated in Fig. 13 . When exposed to UV light, TiO2 became excited, leading to the generation of EHPs. However, the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 was impeded by the rapid recombination of these EHPs. In the case of the UV-CZTs, upon UV light irradiation, the photogenerated electrons of TiO2 were effectively transferred to the surface of the CDs, significantly hindering the recombination of EHPs. The electrons generated in the conduction band (CB) of TiO2 could react with dissolved O2 in the solution, forming reactive superoxide radical anions (·O2 −). By contrast, the photogenerated holes on the valence band (VB) of TiO2 did not primarily react with water to produce active ·OH radicals. Hence, both h+ and ·O2 − were the dominant active species responsible for the degradation of MB. The dispersion of TiO2 on zeolite-4A not only increased the surface area of TiO2, but also prevented its aggregation, resulting in enhanced photocatalytic activity. Moreover, the adsorption of MB on the zeolite-4A can increase the contact possibility with TiO2 photocatalysts, further enhancing the photocatalytic performance.

In this work, we proposed a novel approach for synthesizing a bifunctional composite with adsorption-photocatalysis, denoted as UV-CZTs. The process involved two key steps. (1) The precursors were easily prepared through hydroxyl radical activation under UV irradiation during the aging process, and (2) the composite materials were subsequently synthesized using a hydrothermal method. Extensive characterization analyses were performed to assess the structural and photocatalytic properties of the composites. The results confirmed the successful loading of the TiO2 and coal-based CDs onto zeolite-4A. Compared to the Dark-CZTs, the UV-CZTs exhibited a more complete zeolite-4A matrix, with higher loading of TiO2. The presence of CDs also led to enhanced light absorption and improved photocatalytic performance in the UV-CZTs composite photocatalysts. The photocatalytic kinetics followed the pseudo first-order kinetic equation, indicating the efficient degradation of pollutants. The composite photocatalyst demonstrated high stability, making it a promising candidate for environmental pollutant degradation applications. This study also delved into the photocatalytic mechanism of the composite, offering valuable insights. Overall, this work not only presented a novel approach for designing TiO2-based composite photocatalysts with high activity, but also provided a feasible solution for the valuable utilization of low-quality coal-based and coal-derived resources.
